questions.specification:
**Figure 1**: A system diagram of the global carbon cycle.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-ess-1350b917-f518-4054-b208-4ff8aef9c3f6-1771953681492/813334e1-c953-467d-a3ae-8209aeed4c1d.jpeg)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question is centered on a system diagram of the global carbon cycle, requiring identification of inorganic carbon storages and transformation flows, and impacts of warming on carbon storages.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) test systems concepts: steady-state equilibrium in the atmospheric carbon store and identifying the system type that exchanges energy but not matter (closed system).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) links increased global surface temperatures to changes in carbon storages (climate change impacts interacting with the carbon cycle).

parts.content: Identify **one** storage of inorganic carbon and **one** flow that represents a transformation in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
### Inorganic storage
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$
- Ocean Surface (dissolved inorganic carbon)
- Fossil Fuel Reserves

### Transformation process
<marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following:
- Photosynthesis (conversion of inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ to organic biomass)
- Respiration (conversion of organic biomass to inorganic $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)
- Combustion (chemical change from fuel/biomass to $$\text{CO}_{2}$$)

<note>Do not credit Diffusion/Dissolution as this is a physical transfer (moving matter between phases/locations without chemical change).</note>

parts.content: Outline how a significant increase in global surface temperatures could impact **two** of the carbon storages shown in **Figure 1**.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant impact on a named storage, <marks>2 marks max</marks>:

- **Ocean Surface**: Reduction in carbon storage because warmer water holds less dissolved $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ (decreased solubility).
- **Terrestrial Vegetation**: Reduction in storage due to increased frequency/intensity of wildfires OR plant dieback from heat stress/drought.
- **Atmospheric $$\text{CO}_{2}$$**: Increase in storage as a result of reduced uptake by oceans/vegetation or increased release from permafrost/soils.
- **Fossil Fuel Reserves**: No direct impact from temperature, but may be reduced if warming leads to increased energy demand for cooling (indirect combustion).

<note>Credit can be given for any logically sound impact of temperature on a specific storage box in the diagram. Do not credit impacts on flows unless they are explicitly linked to a change in the storage level.</note>

parts.content: Explain how the atmospheric storage in **Figure 1** could be considered to be in a state of **steady-state equilibrium**.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>3 marks</marks> for a clear explanation:

- Steady-state equilibrium occurs when the total inputs to a storage are equal to the total outputs over a period of time. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In the context of the atmosphere, inputs include processes like respiration and combustion (which add carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Outputs include processes like photosynthesis and diffusion into the ocean (which remove carbon). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Despite constant flows of carbon into and out of the atmosphere, the overall concentration of $$\text{CO}_{2}$$ remains relatively constant/stable. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 3 marks max. The student must demonstrate the concept of balanced inputs/outputs maintaining a stable storage level.</note>

parts.content: State the name of the system type that exchanges energy but not matter with its surroundings.
parts.markscheme:
- Closed system <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not accept "isolated system" (no exchange of energy or matter) or "open system" (exchange of both).</note>

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 5: Land > 5.1 Soil > 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 5.1A Soil system
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The rate of infiltration is primarily determined by soil system characteristics such as soil texture, structure, and organic content, making soil a fundamental factor in this process.

parts.content: Describe two factors that influence the rate of infiltration in a drainage basin.
parts.markscheme:
### Factor 1
- Soil texture/structure <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Sandy/porous soils allow faster infiltration OR
  - Clay/compacted soils slow infiltration <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Factor 2
- Vegetation cover <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - More vegetation increases infiltration by protecting soil and increasing porosity OR
  - Less vegetation reduces infiltration as soil is exposed/compacted <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each other valid factor (max 2):
- Soil moisture content
- Slope angle/gradient
- Rainfall intensity
- Human activities (e.g. compaction by vehicles)

<note>Accept other reasonable responses</note>

parts.content: Describe the concept of a watershed and its importance in water resource management.
parts.markscheme:
A watershed is the area of land draining into a particular river or water body. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Defines the boundaries for water resource management. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Impacts on one part of the watershed can affect the entire system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Important for planning conservation and pollution control measures. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>3 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Identify two major historical events that have influenced the development of the environmental movement and explain how each event contributed to a shift in environmental value systems (EVS).
parts.markscheme:
*   **Event 1: Publication of *Silent Spring* by Rachel Carson (1962)**
    - *Impact*: Raised public awareness of the dangers of chemical pesticides (DDT) on ecosystems / catalyzed the move toward ecocentrism by highlighting human-nature interdependence. <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Event 2: Bhopal Disaster (1984)**
    - *Impact*: A massive gas leak in India that killed thousands / raised awareness of the risks of industrialization and poor technological oversight, shifting perspectives toward more cautious, anthropocentric, or technophobic views. <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Event 3: Chernobyl Nuclear Disaster (1986)**
    - *Impact*: Highlighted the transboundary nature of pollution and the potential global catastrophes associated with high-technology solutions, fostering skepticism toward technocentrism. <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Event 4: Minamata Disaster (1950s)**
    - *Impact*: Linked industrial mercury pollution to human health issues (mercury poisoning), leading to a stronger anthropocentric push for environmental regulation and corporate responsibility. <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Event 5: Gaia Hypothesis (James Lovelock)**
    - *Impact*: Proposed the Earth as a self-regulating organism, reinforcing a deep-ecology/ecocentric perspective where humans are just one part of a larger system. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept any other valid historical event (e.g., Fukushima, Whaling protests, Al Gore’s "An Inconvenient Truth") provided the link to EVS change is clearly explained. Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a relevant event and <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining its impact on EVS, <marks>4 marks max</marks>.

parts.content:
Contrast the perspectives of a 'Soft Technologist' and a 'Cornucopian' regarding the management of global fossil fuel depletion.

To what extent do these two viewpoints reflect different underlying assumptions about the relationship between humans and the environment?

parts.content: Explain the difference between surface runoff and baseflow in a river system.
parts.markscheme:
- Surface runoff is water that flows over the land surface to the river. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Baseflow is the contribution to river flow from groundwater. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Surface runoff responds quickly to precipitation events. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Baseflow provides a more constant supply of water to rivers. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>3 marks max</marks>

parts.markscheme:
- Reduced transpiration → reduced water vapour returned to atmosphere OR reduced evapotranspiration <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Increased surface runoff → reduced interception OR increased soil erosion OR reduced infiltration <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe two types of aquifers and their characteristics.
parts.markscheme:
#### Unconfined aquifer
- Water table forms upper boundary
- Directly recharged by precipitation
<marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Confined aquifer
- Bounded by impermeable layers
- Can be under pressure
<marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Artesian aquifer
- Confined aquifer where water level rises above ground surface <marks>2 marks</marks>
#### Perched aquifer
- Localized aquifer above the main water table, separated by impermeable layer <marks>2 marks</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain how energy drives the hydrological cycle.
parts.markscheme:
- Primary source of energy driving hydrological cycle is solar energy <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes changes of state in water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Condensation of water releases (latent) heat energy causing air to rise/hurricanes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes the temperature differences that create winds/cause movement of water vapour in the atmosphere/advection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Kinetic/wind energy moves clouds (from ocean over land, usually) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy melts ice producing streams/lakes/rising ocean levels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Solar energy causes both transfers (_e.g._ advection) and transformations (_e.g._ evaporation) in hydrological cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Precipitation/run-off/streamflow occurs due to gravitational energy/difference in potential energy between the high and low positions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transpiration is driven by opening of leaf stomata which is due to chemical energy (cellular respiration in mitochondria) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Using a system diagram, explain how human activities affect global water cycle flows.
parts.markscheme:
**Identifying Impacts** <marks>up to 4 marks</marks>
- Melting will increase due to global warming/climate change/increased greenhouse gas emissions
- Freezing will decrease due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Precipitation will be polluted due to acid formation/emissions of NOx & SOx/combustion of coal
- Precipitation could change/increase/decrease due to climate change/etc.
- Percolation/infiltration will decrease due to urbanization/soil compaction caused by overgrazing
- Run-off will increase due to urbanization/deforestation
- Warmer/polluted streams due to thermal/toxic pollution from industrial effluent
- Evaporation will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Evapotranspiration will increase due to global warming/climate change/etc.
- Evapotranspiration will be reduced due to deforestation
- Uptake by plants will be reduced due to deforestation/urbanization/long fallow periods
- Groundwater flow/aquifers will change dependent on climatic impacts/precipitation/rate of human extraction
- Cloud seeding increases precipitation (addressing problem of drought/fog around airports)

**Quality of Diagram** <marks>up to 3 marks</marks>

Award 1 mark for every 3 correctly labeled flows or storages. No marks for less than 3.

<note>Maximum 2 marks if gradient is left as a fraction</note>

parts.content: To what extent do different environmental value systems improve fresh water access?
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 
- Availability & distribution of water
- Water security
- Climate change
- Water pollution
- Sustainable management of freshwater resources
- Reservoirs
- Desalination
- Artificial recharge
- Water conservation
- Environmental value systems
- Increasing demand for water
- Water scarcity
- Link a range of environmental value systems to specific management strategies
- Link environmental value systems to general approaches that improve or hinder widespread access to freshwater
- Consider geographical locations differing in freshwater availability/quality
- Consider societies of different economic development
- Provide examples of strategies and human management activities/approaches linked to specific value systems that impact water accessibility


| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Define the term "hydrosphere" and explain its importance in the Earth system.
parts.markscheme:
The hydrosphere is all water on Earth's surface and in the atmosphere. It includes oceans, rivers, lakes, groundwater, glaciers, and water vapor. <marks>1 mark</marks>

The hydrosphere plays a crucial role in climate regulation and supporting life. <marks>1 mark</marks>

It facilitates the movement of energy and materials around the planet. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Identify **two** major storages of matter that are present in a named aquatic ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Biomass (e.g., fish, plankton, aquatic plants) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved nutrients (e.g., nitrates, phosphates in the water column) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Benthic sediments (e.g., organic matter or minerals on the lake/sea floor) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dissolved gases (e.g., oxygen, carbon dioxide) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The water body itself (as a storage of $$H_2O$$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid storage identified, up to 2 marks. Do not credit flows (e.g., precipitation or runoff) unless they are clearly defined as a storage within the context of the system.</note>

parts.content: State **one** transfer and **one** transformation of matter that occur within a soil system.
parts.markscheme:
- Transfer: Leaching (minerals moving downwards through soil profiles) / Runoff / Infiltration of water / Deposition of leaf litter <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transformation: Decomposition (organic matter turning into inorganic nutrients) / Weathering of parent rock / Nitrification / Denitrification <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks. Ensure one of each (transfer and transformation) is provided for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the use of models (such as systems diagrams or computer simulations) in understanding the complexities of an ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- Models simplify complex systems, making them easier to visualize and study <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They allow scientists to predict future changes or the impacts of human activities (e.g., climate change models) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They serve as effective communication tools for explaining environmental issues to the public or policy-makers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- They can identify gaps in current knowledge or data by highlighting where the model fails to match observations <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, models are often over-simplified and may miss emergent properties or small-scale interactions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The accuracy of a model is limited by the quality of the input data ("garbage in, garbage out") <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Models often cannot account for unpredictable, stochastic events such as natural disasters <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Predictions are often probabilistic and involve a high degree of uncertainty, which can be misinterpreted <marks>1 mark</marks>



<note>Accept any other reasonable evaluation points. </note>

parts.content:
Environmental systems are often characterized by their resilience and state of equilibrium. 

Discuss the role of feedback mechanisms in determining whether a system maintains a stable equilibrium or crosses a tipping point into a new state.

questions.specification:
Analyze the following graph showing per capita water availability in different years:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ad654a74-1200-43dd-8b21-243df62f4e21)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.2 Water access, use and security > 4.2E Freshwater resources and use (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.2E Freshwater resources and use (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The graph concerns per-capita water availability over time; explaining the overall trend typically involves changes in freshwater resources and human use/demand patterns.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.2 Water access, use and security > 4.2C Water scarcity
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.2C Water scarcity
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A declining per-capita availability indicates increasing water scarcity relative to population and withdrawals, which aligns with identifying and explaining scarcity trends.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.2 Water access, use and security > 4.2A Water security
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.2A Water security
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Per-capita availability is a key indicator used to assess water security; the question asks to interpret decline and drivers affecting security.

parts.content: Identify the year with the steepest decline in water availability.
parts.markscheme: Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the year (1991) with the steepest decline.

parts.content: Explain two reasons for the overall trend shown in the graph.
parts.markscheme:
### Reason 1 <marks>2 marks</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a valid reason, for example:
- Population growth/increase in population
- Increased demand for water (for domestic/industrial/agricultural use)
- Climate change/global warming (leading to droughts/reduced rainfall)
- Overexploitation/overconsumption of water resources
- Deforestation/destruction of watersheds
- Pollution/contamination of water sources

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear explanation linking the reason to the decreasing trend in water availability.

### Reason 2 <marks>2 marks</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a different valid reason from the list above.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear explanation linking the reason to the decreasing trend in water availability.

<note>Award a maximum of 2 marks if only one reason is given.</note>

<commonMistake>
Confusing reasons for the overall trend with reasons for the year-to-year fluctuations.
</commonMistake>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1E Thermohaline circulation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1E Thermohaline circulation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The 'great ocean conveyor belt' is the standard term used to describe the thermohaline circulation, which is the primary subject of topic 4.1E. The question specifically asks for the drivers (temperature and salinity gradients) that move this system.

parts.content: Outline the processes that drive the movement of the great ocean conveyor belt.
parts.markscheme:
- Circulation in the ocean is driven by temperature variations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cold water has a lower density, which causes it to sink and hot water to rise. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Salinity also affects ocean circulation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- More saline water will sink, and less saline water will rise. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Differences in temperature and salinity lead to differences in water density, which drive the circulation of water in the ocean conveyor belt. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>4 marks max</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: A drainage basin (catchment) is part of river/hydrological system terminology (inputs, stores, flows, watershed), typically taught within the hydrological cycle/water systems content.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Describing components of a drainage basin can be framed as identifying system components (stores/flows/boundary), linking to general systems concepts, though it is secondary to water systems.

parts.content: Describe three main components of a drainage basin.
parts.markscheme:
- Watershed/divide: The boundary separating different drainage basins.
- River channel: The path water follows as it flows downstream.
- Tributaries: Smaller streams that feed into the main river channel.
- Floodplain: Flat area adjacent to the river that floods during high water.

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correctly described component, up to <marks>3 marks</marks>

questions.specification:
Earth's water is distributed among various storages with vastly different volumes and residence times. While the majority of water is found in the oceans, the small fraction of freshwater is critical for terrestrial life and is redistributed through global systems such as the thermohaline circulation.

**Table 1: Distribution of Earth's Water Storages**

| Storage | Percentage of Total Earth Water (%) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Oceans | 97.41 |
| Ice caps and glaciers | 1.98 |
| Groundwater | 0.59 |
| Lakes | 0.007 |
| Soil moisture | 0.001 |
| Atmosphere | 0.001 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1E Thermohaline circulation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1E Thermohaline circulation (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) explicitly asks about the Global Conveyor Belt (thermohaline circulation) and how density differences drive it.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The data table and Part (0) focus on identifying major freshwater reservoirs (ice caps/glaciers and groundwater), which relates to storages within the hydrological cycle and global water distribution.

parts.content: Using the data provided in Table 1, identify the two largest reservoirs of freshwater on Earth.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correctly identified storage, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:
- Ice caps and glaciers
- Groundwater

<note>Do not accept 'Oceans' as they are a saltwater storage.</note>

parts.content: Explain how differences in water density drive the movement of the 'Global Conveyor Belt' (thermohaline circulation).
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying the cause of density changes and <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing the resulting flow, up to <marks>2 marks</marks>:
- Variations in temperature (thermal) and salinity (haline) cause water to become more dense, particularly in polar regions where it cools and salt is concentrated by ice formation.
- This dense water sinks (downwelling) to the ocean floor, creating a deep-water current that pulls in warmer surface water from the tropics to replace it, driving global circulation.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about impacts of climate change on the global water cycle, which directly concerns processes/components of the hydrological cycle (evaporation, precipitation, runoff, storage).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 6: Atmosphere and climate change > 6.2 Climate change - causes and impact > 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.2B Climate change
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: It specifically frames the changes as consequences of climate change, so it also assesses understanding of climate change impacts.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which climate change might impact the global water cycle.
parts.markscheme:
#### Method #1

- Increased evaporation rates due to higher temperatures leading to more moisture in the atmosphere <marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Method #2

- Changes in precipitation patterns, with some areas becoming wetter and others drier <marks>2 marks</marks>

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1A Hydrological cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Infiltration and percolation are key processes within the hydrological cycle describing movement of water into soil and downward to aquifers, directly relating to groundwater recharge.

parts.content: Compare and contrast infiltration and percolation in terms of their roles in groundwater recharge.
parts.markscheme:
### Infiltration
- Infiltration is the entry of water into the soil surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It is the first stage in groundwater recharge <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Percolation
- Percolation is the downward movement of water within the soil and underlying rock <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It allows water to reach the water table and recharge groundwater supplies

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid comparison/contrast of the roles of infiltration and percolation in groundwater recharge. Maximum 3 marks.</note>

questions.specification:
**Figure 1: Taiga**

*   Taiga is the world’s largest terrestrial biome.
*   The Siberian taiga contains about 20 % of the world’s forested areas.
*   The taiga includes evergreen trees such as pine, spruce, fir, and some deciduous trees such as birch and poplar.
*   Nutrient-poor soil and the climatic conditions contribute to slow rate of tree growth.
*   Threats to taiga include forest fires, clearance for agriculture land, logging for timber, mining, road building and climate change.
*   The region suffers deforestation rates of 20000km2 annually.
*   Conservation measures include restoration of forests through replanting and designating protected areas such as National Parks and selective logging policies.
*   There is high global demand for timber with markets throughout Asia, Europe and the USA. Economic incentives to increase exports have increased timber production, destroying more of the forest ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 7: Natural resources > 7.1 Natural resources - uses and management > 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for an ecological service provided by the taiga, which directly targets ecosystem services.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Deforestation affecting the world’s oceans is most plausibly via increased atmospheric CO2/reduced carbon sequestration, linking terrestrial forests to the carbon cycle and ocean uptake.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly connects land deforestation to impacts on the oceans, commonly explained through altered CO2 levels and oceans’ role as a carbon sink (ocean acidification).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 3: Conservation and biodiversity > 3.3 Conservation and regeneration > 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) concerns restoration through replanting and what limits regeneration, fitting regeneration/restoration concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The stimulus and questions are set in the taiga biome context (climate/soil constraints), so understanding biomes underpins limiting factors and services.

parts.content: Outline **one** ecological service provided by the Siberian taiga.
Explain how deforestation in the taiga may impact the world’s oceans.
State **one** natural limiting factor to the successful restoration of taiga through replanting.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following (<marks>1 max</marks>):

nutrient poor soils / low temperature / low rainfall / limited growing season / limited amount of sunlight / slow growth rates / permafrost inhibits plant growth;

_Do not accept ‘wildfires / grazers/animals eat the plants/seedlings’._  
_Do not accept just ‘temperature/rainfall/soil fertility/time it takes for trees to grow/long mature time for trees’._

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.4 Water pollution > 4.4B Water quality
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4B Water quality
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for methods to assess water quality in a river system, which directly corresponds to the 'Water quality' sub-topic that covers biological (e.g., biotic indices), chemical (e.g., dissolved oxygen, pH), and physical (e.g., turbidity) indicators of water health.

parts.content: Describe two ways in which water quality can be assessed in a river system.
parts.markscheme:
#### Method #1

Chemical analysis: Measure parameters like ${pH}$, dissolved oxygen, nutrients

Dissolved oxygen levels indicate the amount of oxygen available for aquatic life

pH measures acidity/alkalinity which affects biological processes

Nutrient levels like nitrates and phosphates can lead to eutrophication

<marks>2 marks</marks>

#### Method #2

Biological indicators: Use presence/absence of certain species to indicate water quality

- Some organisms are sensitive to pollution, their absence suggests poor water quality
- Other organisms are tolerant of pollution, their presence indicates contamination

<marks>2 marks</marks>
